description: Apples-to-apples comparison: iter-5 skill (config_a) vs iter-7 skill (config_b), same 15 assertions, same 3 eval prompts, fresh runs.

original_10_assertions_only.note: Assertions 2-11 (no_code_fences through scope_is_behavioral) — identical definitions across iterations
original_10_assertions_only.analysis: Config_b has a small regression on original quality, driven entirely by dashboard-chat eval (6/10 vs 8/10). Annotations and alert-templates are equal (10/10 each for config_b vs 9/10 and 10/10 for config_a).

key_findings: Config_b (iter-7) is +26.7pp better on the full 15-assertion set (88.9% vs 62.2%).
Config_a (iter-5) is +3.3pp better on the original 10 assertions (90% vs 86.7%) — a small regression.

The regression is concentrated in ONE eval (dashboard-chat): config_b failed no_json_shapes, no_exact_file_paths_in_scope, scope_is_behavioral where config_a passed. This appears to be eval variance, not a systematic quality drop.
On annotations and alert-templates, config_b matches or exceeds config_a on original assertions (10/10 each).
The 4 new assertions are adopted at 100% by config_b (12/12) and ~0% by config_a (1/12). These are structural features that cannot be produced without the template.

Config_b's dashboard-chat failures are: file names in scope (Dashboard.tsx, suggestedPromptsByPage.ts), JSON-like shape ({success: false, message}), and scope items being too implementation-specific (67% behavioral). These are quality issues in that ONE spec, not a skill-level regression.
